era_context: The Late Bronze Age in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. The New Kingdom of Egypt under the 18th Dynasty (Thutmose III era or immediate successors) was consolidating imperial power in the Levant. The Hittite Empire was in its early New Kingdom phase. Mycenaean Greece was in the Late Helladic II period. In Mesopotamia, the Kassite dynasty controlled Babylonia. This was an era of intense interstate diplomacy, military campaigns, and trade network expansion.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological record for this specific year)
East Asia (Chinese records exist but are not well-synchronized with this calendar year)
Oceania and the Pacific (no written records)

events.title: Thutmose III's campaigns in the Levant (ongoing)
events.description: Thutmose III of Egypt continued his systematic military campaigns to consolidate Egyptian control over Levantine territories and suppress local rebellions. These campaigns, documented in temple inscriptions at Karnak, were part of a broader strategy to establish Egyptian hegemony over the region and secure trade routes. The campaigns involved multiple sieges and battles against coalitions of Levantine city-states and were crucial to establishing Egypt as a dominant imperial power.

sources.name: Karnak Temple Inscriptions (Annals of Thutmose III)

events.certainty_note: Thutmose III's reign is well-documented, but precise dating of individual campaigns within his 54-year reign remains uncertain. This year falls within his documented campaign period (c. 1479–1425 BCE), but specific events for 1510 BCE are not independently confirmed.

events.title: Hittite consolidation under Tudhaliya I or successor
events.description: The Hittite New Kingdom was consolidating power in Anatolia and expanding influence into northern Syria. This period saw the development of the Hittite imperial administrative system and military organization. The exact ruler and specific events for 1510 BCE are not clearly attested, but the broader era was one of Hittite expansion and state-building.

sources.name: Hittite royal inscriptions and clay tablets from Boğazköy

events.certainty_note: Hittite chronology for this period is debated. The conventional 'middle chronology' places this in the early New Kingdom, but the 'low chronology' would shift dates significantly. No specific events are attested for this exact year.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Thutmose III's reign itself is subject to ongoing scholarly debate. Some scholars argue for a 'low chronology' that would place events attributed to 1510 BCE several decades later. Additionally, the Hittite chronology for this period remains contested, with alternative chronological systems producing significantly different date assignments. The significance of events in this year cannot be independently verified without resolving these broader chronological disputes.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1510 BCE is heavily dependent on Egyptian temple inscriptions and Hittite clay tablets, both of which are court-sponsored sources reflecting elite perspectives. Egyptian records tend to emphasize military victories and downplay defeats. Hittite records are fragmentary and often difficult to date precisely. Archaeological evidence from the Levant provides some independent corroboration but is not always datable to specific years. The absence of contemporary records from many regions (Mesopotamia, Greece, India) means that a truly global history for this year is impossible. Chronological uncertainty is the dominant problem: without resolving debates over absolute dating systems, the assignment of events to specific years remains provisional.

graph_edges.note: Thutmose III's campaigns continued across multiple years, establishing Egyptian hegemony
